quiz_questions.question_text: What caused the devastating 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami?

options.text: A massive underwater volcanic eruption
A large submarine landslide in the Bay of Bengal
A meteor impact in the Indian Ocean
A magnitude 9.1 earthquake at a subduction zone off Sumatra

quiz_questions.explanation: The 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami was triggered by a magnitude 9.1 megathrust earthquake along the Sunda Trench, where the Indian Plate subducts beneath the Burma Plate. The sudden vertical displacement of the seafloor displaced a massive volume of water, generating the deadly tsunami that killed over 230,000 people.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Hawaiian Islands were formed by:

options.text: A convergent plate boundary creating an island arc
An ancient continental fragment left behind as plates separated
A stationary hotspot beneath a moving Pacific Plate
A mid-ocean ridge emerging above sea level

quiz_questions.explanation: The Hawaiian Islands formed as the Pacific Plate moved northwest over a stationary mantle hotspot. Each island formed over the hotspot, then was carried away as the plate moved, creating a chain of progressively older islands. The Big Island (Hawaii) is currently over the hotspot and is the youngest.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is Iceland geologically unique?

options.text: It is made entirely of sedimentary rock
It sits on both a mid-ocean ridge and a mantle hotspot, making the ridge visible above sea level
It is the only island formed by a transform fault
It sits at the junction of three tectonic plates

quiz_questions.explanation: Iceland straddles the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and sits over a deep mantle hotspot. This combination produces an unusually high volume of volcanic material, making Iceland one of the very few places on Earth where a mid-ocean ridge rises above sea level. The island experiences frequent volcanic eruptions and geothermal activity.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Challenger Deep, the deepest point in the ocean, was first reached by humans in which year?
quiz_questions.explanation: In 1960, Jacques Piccard and Don Walsh descended to the bottom of the Challenger Deep in the bathyscaphe Trieste, reaching approximately 10,916 meters depth. This historic dive proved that life exists at the deepest point in the ocean. In 2012, James Cameron made the first solo dive to the Challenger Deep.

quiz_questions.question_text: What was the Messinian Salinity Crisis?

options.text: A volcanic event that heated the Indian Ocean to dangerous levels
A period when the Mediterranean Sea was isolated from the Atlantic and largely evaporated
A time when the Atlantic Ocean became too salty for marine life
A freshwater flood that diluted the Pacific Ocean

quiz_questions.explanation: The Messinian Salinity Crisis (about 5.96–5.33 million years ago) occurred when tectonic activity closed the Strait of Gibraltar, isolating the Mediterranean Sea from the Atlantic. The sea partially or fully evaporated multiple times, depositing massive layers of salt and gypsum on its floor.

quiz_questions.question_text: What geological process is responsible for the existence of offshore oil and gas deposits?

options.text: Burial and thermal maturation of organic-rich marine sediments over millions of years
Decomposition of coral reefs into petroleum
Volcanic eruptions that trapped gas in lava flows
Chemical precipitation of hydrocarbons from seawater

quiz_questions.explanation: Offshore oil and gas form when organic-rich marine sediments (from dead plankton and other organisms) are buried under thick layers of additional sediment. Over millions of years, heat and pressure transform the organic matter into hydrocarbons (oil and natural gas) that migrate into porous reservoir rocks.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the Hawaiian hotspot chain, which island is the oldest?

options.text: Big Island (Hawaii)
Kauai
Oahu
Maui

quiz_questions.explanation: Among the main Hawaiian Islands, Kauai is the oldest at approximately 5.1 million years old. The islands get progressively younger toward the southeast, with the Big Island (Hawaii) being the youngest at less than 0.7 million years old. This age progression reflects the northwest movement of the Pacific Plate over the stationary hotspot.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami affected coastlines in how many countries?

options.text: 3 countries
2 countries
14 countries
7 countries

quiz_questions.explanation: The 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami affected coastlines in 14 countries around the Indian Ocean, including Indonesia, Sri Lanka, India, Thailand, and Somalia. It traveled across the entire Indian Ocean basin, with waves reaching as far as East Africa, demonstrating the immense energy released by the underwater earthquake.

quiz_questions.question_text: What type of plate boundary is found where the 2004 Indian Ocean earthquake originated?

options.text: Divergent boundary
Transform boundary
Convergent boundary (subduction zone)
Hotspot

quiz_questions.explanation: The 2004 earthquake occurred at the Sunda megathrust, a convergent boundary where the Indian-Australian Plate subducts beneath the Eurasian Plate along the Sunda Trench. Megathrust earthquakes at subduction zones are the most powerful earthquakes on Earth and the most common cause of destructive tsunamis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What submarine feature runs through the center of Iceland?

options.text: The Mariana Trench
The East Pacific Rise
The Java Trench
The Mid-Atlantic Ridge

quiz_questions.explanation: The Mid-Atlantic Ridge runs directly through Iceland, making it one of the few places on Earth where a mid-ocean ridge is exposed above sea level. The ridge divides Iceland into the North American Plate to the west and the Eurasian Plate to the east, and can be observed at the Thingvellir rift valley.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'megathrust earthquake'?

options.text: Any earthquake above magnitude 5.0
An earthquake at a transform boundary
A deep-focus earthquake beneath a mid-ocean ridge
An extremely powerful earthquake that occurs at a subduction zone along the megathrust fault

quiz_questions.explanation: A megathrust earthquake occurs along the interface (megathrust fault) between the subducting and overriding plates at a convergent boundary. These are the most powerful earthquakes on Earth, capable of exceeding magnitude 9.0. They can cause massive tsunamis by vertically displacing the ocean floor.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'mantle plume' in the context of hotspot geology?

options.text: A plume of smoke from an underwater volcanic eruption
A sediment cloud from a submarine landslide
A column of abnormally hot rock that rises from deep within the Earth's mantle to the surface
A surface lava flow from a shield volcano

quiz_questions.explanation: A mantle plume is a column of exceptionally hot rock that rises from deep within the Earth's mantle, possibly from near the core-mantle boundary. When a plume reaches the base of the lithosphere, it causes melting and volcanic activity, creating hotspot volcanoes like those forming the Hawaiian Island chain.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the geological term for thick salt deposits formed by evaporation, like those found under the Mediterranean?

options.text: Evaporites
Turbidites
Carbonates
Basalts

quiz_questions.explanation: Evaporites are chemical sedimentary deposits formed when bodies of water evaporate, leaving behind dissolved minerals like halite (rock salt) and gypsum. The Mediterranean evaporite deposits from the Messinian Salinity Crisis are among the largest on Earth, with salt layers up to 3 km thick in places.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'hotspot track' refer to?

options.text: A zone of elevated ocean temperatures caused by currents
The thermal signature of a hydrothermal vent field
The path of a hurricane across the ocean surface
A chain of progressively older volcanoes formed as a tectonic plate moves over a stationary hotspot

quiz_questions.explanation: A hotspot track is the chain of volcanoes (islands or seamounts) created as a tectonic plate moves over a stationary mantle hotspot. The volcanoes become progressively older and more eroded with distance from the current hotspot location. The Hawaiian-Emperor seamount chain is a classic example.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'offshore drilling' in marine geology?

options.text: Using sonar to locate resources beneath the seafloor
Drilling into the ocean floor from ships or platforms to extract oil, gas, or scientific cores
Drilling on land near the coast to find underground water
Mining coral reefs for construction materials

quiz_questions.explanation: Offshore drilling involves using specialized ships, platforms, or rigs to drill wells into the ocean floor. This technology is used both commercially (to extract oil and natural gas) and scientifically (such as the Ocean Drilling Program) to recover sediment and rock cores for geological research.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'shield volcano'?

options.text: A steep, cone-shaped volcano with explosive eruptions
A broad, gently sloping volcano built by low-viscosity lava flows
A submarine volcano that never reaches the surface
A volcanic crater that has collapsed into a caldera

quiz_questions.explanation: A shield volcano is a broad, dome-shaped volcano with gently sloping sides, built mainly by the flow of low-viscosity basaltic lava. Hawaiian volcanoes like Mauna Loa are classic examples of shield volcanoes, which are the largest volcanoes on Earth by volume.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'tsunami' literally mean in Japanese?

options.text: Sea surge
Tidal flood
Giant wave
Harbor wave

quiz_questions.explanation: Tsunami is a Japanese word meaning 'harbor wave' (tsu = harbor, nami = wave). The name originates from Japanese fishermen who returned to port to find the harbor devastated by a large wave, even though they had not noticed anything unusual while at sea in deep water.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'Zanclean flood' in the context of Mediterranean geology?

options.text: A period when the Mediterranean slowly filled from rainfall
The catastrophic refilling of the Mediterranean basin when the Strait of Gibraltar reopened
An underwater volcanic eruption that flooded coastal cities
An ancient tsunami that devastated Mediterranean coastlines

quiz_questions.explanation: The Zanclean flood (about 5.33 million years ago) was the dramatic refilling of the Mediterranean basin when the Strait of Gibraltar reopened, ending the Messinian Salinity Crisis. Atlantic waters may have poured in as a massive waterfall, refilling the basin in as little as a few months to a few thousand years.

quiz_questions.question_text: The term 'bathyscaphe' refers to:

options.text: A tool used for echo sounding
A free-diving, self-propelled deep-sea submersible designed for great depths
A type of underwater camera
A remotely operated vehicle for sampling sediments

quiz_questions.explanation: A bathyscaphe is a type of deep-diving submersible consisting of a crew cabin attached beneath a float filled with a lighter-than-water liquid (gasoline). The bathyscaphe Trieste set the record for the deepest dive when it reached the Challenger Deep in 1960.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'Loihi' refer to in the Hawaiian hotspot chain?

options.text: An ancient caldera on Maui
A submarine volcano forming southeast of the Big Island that will become the next Hawaiian island
The oldest island in the chain
A coral reef surrounding Kauai

quiz_questions.explanation: Loihi is an active submarine volcano located about 35 km southeast of the Big Island of Hawaii. It sits over the Hawaiian hotspot and is currently about 975 meters below sea level. Continued volcanic activity will eventually cause it to rise above sea level, making it the newest Hawaiian island in tens of thousands of years.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami traveled across the open ocean at speeds comparable to a commercial jet airplane. Why were the waves not noticed by ships at sea?

options.text: The waves were traveling underwater and could not be detected at the surface
Ships were warned in advance and moved out of the wave path
In deep water, tsunami waves have very long wavelengths but very small wave heights, making them imperceptible to ships
The waves only formed when they reached shallow coastal waters

quiz_questions.explanation: In deep ocean water, a tsunami has an extremely long wavelength (100–200 km) but a wave height of only about 0.5–1 meter. Ships experience only a gentle, slow rise and fall that is undetectable. As the wave enters shallow coastal waters, the wavelength compresses and the wave height increases dramatically.

quiz_questions.question_text: Scientists determine that the Hawaiian Island chain bends at the location of the Emperor Seamounts. What does this bend tell us about the Pacific Plate's history?

options.text: An asteroid impact diverted the seamount chain
The Pacific Plate changed its direction of movement approximately 47 million years ago
Two separate hotspots created the bend
The hotspot moved to a new location

quiz_questions.explanation: The Hawaiian-Emperor bend, which occurred approximately 47 million years ago, records a major change in the direction of Pacific Plate motion from roughly northward to northwestward. The hotspot remained stationary while the plate changed course, creating the distinctive bend in the volcanic chain.

quiz_questions.question_text: After the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami, what major warning system improvement was implemented in the Indian Ocean region?

options.text: An Indian Ocean Tsunami Warning System with deep-ocean buoys and seismic monitoring was established
All coastal populations were permanently relocated inland
A network of undersea walls was built to block future tsunamis
Satellites were launched that can predict earthquakes before they occur

quiz_questions.explanation: Following the 2004 disaster, the Indian Ocean Tsunami Warning System (IOTWS) was established, incorporating deep-ocean pressure sensors (DART buoys), coastal tide gauges, and seismic monitoring networks. This system can detect potential tsunamis and issue warnings to coastal populations within minutes of an earthquake.

quiz_questions.question_text: A geologist studying ice cores and Mediterranean sediment cores finds evidence that the Mediterranean evaporated and refilled multiple times during the Messinian Salinity Crisis. What would be the geological evidence for these cycles?

options.text: Uniform clay deposits throughout the core
A single thick layer of salt with no interruptions
Layers of volcanic ash alternating with coral reefs
Alternating layers of evaporite deposits (salt, gypsum) and marine sediments

quiz_questions.explanation: Multiple evaporation-refilling cycles would produce alternating layers of evaporite minerals (deposited during evaporation) and normal marine sediments (deposited when the sea refilled). This cyclical pattern in the sediment record is exactly what geologists observe in Mediterranean drill cores.

quiz_questions.question_text: A city on the Pacific coast is located 200 km from a subduction zone trench. An earthquake of magnitude 8.5 occurs on the subduction fault. Approximately how much time would the city have before a potential tsunami arrives?

options.text: About 1 week
Less than 1 minute
About 15–30 minutes, because the tsunami would travel at roughly 700 km/h in the open ocean
Several hours

quiz_questions.explanation: Tsunamis travel at speeds proportional to the square root of ocean depth. In the open ocean (average depth ~4,000 m), this is roughly 700–800 km/h. At 200 km distance, the wave would arrive in about 15–20 minutes. This is why rapid warning systems and evacuation plans are critical for near-source coastlines.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Challenger Deep was measured at approximately 10,994 meters. Jacques Piccard and Don Walsh reported seeing a flatfish on the bottom during their 1960 dive, though this observation has been debated. What environmental conditions make it surprising that any fish would survive there?

options.text: Toxic gases from volcanic activity make the water uninhabitable
Strong currents at that depth would sweep organisms away
The extreme salinity would dehydrate any fish
Water pressure exceeds 1,000 atmospheres and there is complete darkness, near-freezing temperatures, and extremely limited food supply

quiz_questions.explanation: At nearly 11,000 meters, the water pressure is about 1,100 atmospheres, the temperature is just above freezing, there is no light, and food is extremely scarce. Any organism surviving there must have extraordinary adaptations: flexible cell membranes, pressure-resistant proteins, and extremely efficient metabolism.

quiz_questions.question_text: An oil company proposes drilling in a continental shelf area where seismic surveys show dome-shaped structures in the sedimentary layers. A geologist explains these structures could indicate trapped oil. Why do dome-shaped structures trap oil?

options.text: Oil is heavier than surrounding rock and sinks into dome shapes
Oil migrates upward through porous rock and accumulates beneath an impermeable cap rock forming the dome
Domes are ancient coral reefs that converted directly into oil
Volcanic heat beneath domes generates new oil

quiz_questions.explanation: Oil, being less dense than water, migrates upward through porous rock layers. When it encounters an impermeable cap rock folded into a dome (anticlinal trap), it accumulates beneath the dome because it cannot pass through. Geologists use seismic surveys to locate these trap structures for potential drilling.

quiz_questions.question_text: Geologists estimate that Loihi Seamount (the next Hawaiian island) will emerge above sea level in 10,000 to 100,000 years. Currently at about 975 m below sea level, what does this timeline tell us about the volcanic growth rate?

options.text: Loihi is growing at less than 1 mm per year
Loihi is growing at a rate of about 1 to 10 cm per year
Loihi is growing at a rate of over 100 meters per year
Loihi has stopped growing and will emerge only through sea level drop

quiz_questions.explanation: To grow 975 m in 10,000 to 100,000 years requires a rate of about 975 m / 10,000 yr ≈ 9.75 cm/yr to 975 m / 100,000 yr ≈ 0.975 cm/yr, or roughly 1–10 cm per year. This is consistent with the volcanic growth rates observed at other Hawaiian volcanoes.

quiz_questions.question_text: During the Messinian Salinity Crisis, the Mediterranean was cut off from the Atlantic. What tectonic process closed the connection at what is now the Strait of Gibraltar?

options.text: Convergence of the African and Eurasian plates uplifted the land bridge between the two basins
A mid-ocean ridge rose above sea level in the strait
Volcanic eruptions built an island chain blocking the strait
An asteroid impact blocked the waterway with debris

quiz_questions.explanation: The closure of the Strait of Gibraltar was caused by the convergence of the African and Eurasian tectonic plates, which uplifted the land between the Atlantic and Mediterranean basins. This tectonic collision also formed mountain ranges in southern Europe and North Africa.

quiz_questions.question_text: A team of scientists compares the ages of seamounts in the Hawaiian-Emperor chain using radiometric dating. They find that seamounts increase in age by approximately 8.6 million years for every 1,000 km of distance from the current hotspot. What is the approximate rate of Pacific Plate movement over the hotspot?

options.text: About 1.2 cm/year
About 11.6 cm/year
About 50 cm/year
About 7 cm/year

quiz_questions.explanation: Rate = distance / time = 1,000 km / 8.6 million years = 116.3 km per million years ≈ 11.6 cm per year. This calculation demonstrates how hotspot tracks can be used to determine the speed and direction of tectonic plate motion over geological time.
